message.content:
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Passing tests: 8 (all quote base-premium + rune-block tests, now including the always-on +10% first-insurance policy modifier).

Recent Green phase: Added FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE=0.1; quote now computes policyBase = totalBasePremium(items), adds policyModifiers(policyBase) = 0.1*policyBase (first-insurance, applies to every quote), adds PROCESSING_FEE, and rounds the final premium UP with Math.ceil (rounding in MHPCO's favor).

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Run `pnpm test` to verify (must stay 8 passed, 32 todo).

ABSOLUTELY CRITICAL: src/claim-office.ts and the spec file must contain ONLY valid TypeScript. Do NOT write ANY prose, notes, commentary, or summary text into either source file. Put ALL of your summary/report ONLY in your returned message to me. Before returning, run `pnpm test` and confirm it passes. If you accidentally typed any non-code text into the file, remove it and re-run tests.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

export function quote(items: readonly QuoteItem[]): number {
  const policyBase = totalBasePremium(items);
  const withModifiers = policyBase + policyModifiers(policyBase);
  return Math.ceil(withModifiers + PROCESSING_FEE);

content.text:
My earlier Edit failed because the actual code uses arrow-function style and `input: QuoteInput`, not the signature I guessed. The baseline is confirmed green (8 passed, 32 todo). Now let me refactor against the real code.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing `src/claim-office.ts` (lines 49-55):

```ts
const policyModifiers = (policyBase: number): number =>
  FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE * policyBase;
```

- Current name: `policyModifiers`
- Function purpose: computes the first-insurance surcharge (10% of policy base) — and *only* that, currently.
- Question: Does `policyModifiers` reveal intent? Assessment: No. The plural noun `policyModifiers` suggests it aggregates *multiple* policy-wide modifiers (loyalty discount, follow-up discount, first-insurance), but it computes exactly one: the first-insurance surcharge. The name over-promises and the math (`FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE * policyBase`) reveals the actual single concept.
- Decision: Rename to `firstInsuranceSurcharge` to reveal its current, specific intent (Rule 2). This is the highest-value change. The body's operand order is also flipped to `policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` to read as "the base, surcharged" (subject-first), matching the call site's mental model.

## Step 2: Initial APP Mass (the function being changed)

`policyModifiers`: bindings `policyBase` (1) + invocation `*` (2) + binding `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`... constant ref counts as 1 = total 4. Renaming does not change mass; this is a pure Rule 2 (intent) improvement, which correctly trumps APP.

Let me apply the rename to the definition and call site.

content.text:
Tests stay green (8 passed, 32 todo). No stale references to the old name remain. The source file contains only valid TypeScript — no prose was written into it.

## Refactoring Report

### Step 3: Simple Design Rules evaluation

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** Green before and after — 8 passed, 32 todo. Never broken.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Applied. The naming evaluation found `policyModifiers` over-promising — its plural noun implied an aggregate of several policy-wide modifiers, but it computes exactly one thing: the 10% first-insurance surcharge. Renamed to `firstInsuranceSurcharge`. Also introduced an explaining variable `premiumBeforeFee` in `quote`, so the formula reads as two clear stages (apply surcharge to base, then add the fee and round) instead of one dense expression.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication):** None found. `unitPriceFor`, `isRune`, `runeBasePremium`, `sumUnitPrices`, `countRunes`, `totalBasePremium` each carry a single concept with no copy-paste.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No unnecessary abstraction added. The new `premiumBeforeFee` binding earns its place by naming the intermediate concept; it does not over-engineer.

### Step 4-5: APP mass

The renamed function's body: `policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` — 2 bindings + 1 invocation = mass 4, unchanged by the rename. `quote` gains one binding (`premiumBeforeFee`, +1) but the expression it captures is the same set of operations relocated, so total operational mass is effectively flat. This is deliberate: **Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP**, and the change buys intent-revealing names at no real mass cost.

### Changes applied
- Renamed `policyModifiers` → `firstInsuranceSurcharge` (definition + call site).
- Flipped operand order to `policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (subject-first reads as "the base, surcharged").
- Extracted `premiumBeforeFee` explaining variable in `quote`.

### Why this was the right scope
The current code is small and already well-factored (good helper decomposition, named constants, no duplication). The single highest-value improvement available was the misleading name, which matters now because the test list (lines 68-71) shows loyalty and follow-up discounts arriving soon — at which point a *real* `policyModifiers` aggregator may legitimately exist. Freeing that name now prevents a future collision and keeps each function honest about what it currently does.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_22-17-53_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

**Refactoring:** renamed `policyModifiers` → `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, added `premiumBeforeFee` explaining variable.
**Mass change:** ~unchanged (rename is mass-neutral; +1 binding for clarity). Rule 2 over APP, intentionally.
**Tests:** All passing (8 passed, 32 todo).
